questions.q: What is Module Federation / micro-frontends, and when would you use them?

answer.oneLiner: Micro-frontends split a large UI into smaller apps that are built and deployed independently by separate teams. Module Federation is the webpack 5 feature that lets a host app load another app's code at runtime without rebuilding.
answer.detail: Under the hood, Module Federation works in two roles. The remote app exposes specific modules in its webpack config using the exposes key. The host app declares what it will consume using the remotes key. At runtime the host downloads only the remote chunk it needs. Shared dependencies like Angular or React are declared in the shared config so both apps negotiate a single copy to avoid loading the framework twice. When a user navigates to a route owned by the checkout team, the host fetches that team's bundle from their CDN, mounts it, and the user never notices they crossed a team boundary. Simplified: think of it as a dynamic import() on a URL that belongs to a different deployment. In production each team has their own CI/CD pipeline, their own versioning, and their own release cycle.
keyPoints: Each team owns, builds, and deploys their slice completely independently.
The host loads remote chunks at runtime using dynamic URLs, not at build time.
Shared dependencies are deduplicated via the shared config to avoid loading Angular twice.
Use this only when multiple teams need independent release cadences - overkill for a single team.

code.note: The host references the checkout team's remoteEntry.js URL - each team deploys independently and the host picks it up at runtime.

answer.analogy: The host is a stage, each remote is a guest band with their own equipment.
redFlags: Saying micro-frontends are the right choice for a small single-team project.
Not knowing how shared dependencies are deduplicated - leads to shipping Angular twice.
Confusing standalone components with micro-frontends - they are completely different concepts.
Not knowing the difference between host and remote roles in Module Federation.

followUpsQA.q: What are the main downsides of micro-frontends?
followUpsQA.a: Operational complexity increases significantly - you need versioning agreements, cross-team contracts, and shared design system governance. Version skew between remotes can also break the host silently.

followUpsQA.q: How do you prevent two teams from loading different versions of Angular?
followUpsQA.a: List Angular in the shared config with singleton: true and strictVersion: true so Module Federation enforces a single shared copy and errors if versions mismatch.

followUpsQA.q: What is Native Federation and why does it matter?
followUpsQA.a: Native Federation brings Module Federation semantics to the Angular CLI esbuild builder, so you get the same runtime sharing without a webpack-specific config.

followUpsQA.q: How do micro-frontends handle cross-app communication?
followUpsQA.a: Common approaches include a shared event bus on the window object, a published-events contract, or a shared state store served from a singleton remote.

followUpsQA.q: How would you handle authentication tokens across remotes?
followUpsQA.a: Store the token in a singleton auth service provided by the host shell so all remotes share the same instance and read the same token without each implementing their own auth flow.

dialogue.text: What are micro-frontends and Module Federation, and when would you actually use them?

Micro-frontends split a large UI into smaller apps that teams own and deploy independently. Module Federation is the webpack 5 mechanism that lets a host app load another team's bundle from a URL at runtime - so the checkout team can push a fix without the shell team rebuilding anything.
How do you stop Angular from being loaded twice when the host and remote both need it?

You declare it in the shared config with singleton: true and strictVersion: true on both sides. Module Federation then negotiates a single shared instance. If the versions conflict it throws at bootstrap rather than silently loading two copies.
What happens at runtime when a user navigates to the checkout route?

The host's router resolves the route to a lazy loadRemoteModule() call. The browser fetches remoteEntry.js from the checkout team's CDN, executes it, and the Angular router renders the CartComponent as if it were local. The user sees no difference.
You mentioned independent deployments - what is the biggest operational risk there?

Version skew. If the checkout team upgrades to a breaking API change while the host still expects the old contract, the host silently gets the new bundle and breaks at runtime. You need strong integration testing and versioned contracts between teams to catch this.
When would you say no to this architecture?

For a single team or a small app. The added build tooling, cross-team contracts, and runtime coordination overhead are only worth it when you have multiple teams that genuinely need independent release cadences. A well-structured monorepo with lazy routes achieves most of the benefits without the complexity.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What is the virtual DOM in React, and how does it differ from Angular's approach?

answer.oneLiner: React keeps an in-memory copy of the UI called the virtual DOM, compares it with the previous copy on each state change, and applies only the differences to the real DOM. Angular skips the virtual tree entirely and compiles templates into precise DOM update instructions.
answer.detail: When you call setState or update a hook value in React, the component re-renders in memory first. React's reconciler, known as Fiber since React 16, diffs the new virtual DOM tree against the previous one using a heuristic algorithm. It assumes same-type elements stay in place, and uses the key prop to match list items. Only the changed nodes are written to the real DOM. Angular takes a different path. Ivy compiles each template at build time into a sequence of JavaScript instructions like elementStart, textInterpolate, and elementEnd. When change detection runs, those instructions execute and update only the nodes they own. There is no intermediate tree to allocate, diff, or garbage collect. The practical result is that Angular uses less memory for rendering because there is no shadow copy of the DOM. React's VDOM approach gives component authors a simple mental model where they describe the full desired state on each render. Neither is universally faster - it depends on the workload.
keyPoints: React's virtual DOM is a lightweight JS object tree that mirrors the real DOM structure.
Reconciliation diffs the new VDOM against the old and batches minimal real-DOM writes.
Angular's Ivy compiles templates to DOM instructions at build time - no virtual tree exists at runtime.
Angular's incremental DOM approach uses less memory because it allocates no shadow copy.

code.note: Direct DOM mutation bypasses reconciliation - always let the framework manage DOM writes.

answer.analogy: React sketches the full picture in pencil first, then erases only what changed on the canvas.
redFlags: Saying the virtual DOM is always faster than the real DOM - it is a tradeoff, not a guarantee.
Claiming Angular uses a virtual DOM - it does not, Ivy uses incremental DOM instructions.
Not knowing what reconciliation means or how React's Fiber scheduler relates to it.
Confusing the key prop in React lists with Angular's trackBy - they solve the same problem differently.

followUpsQA.q: What is React's reconciliation algorithm?
followUpsQA.a: It is the diffing process where React compares the new virtual DOM tree against the previous one using heuristics - same element type means update in place, different type means unmount and remount.

followUpsQA.q: Why do keys matter in React lists?
followUpsQA.a: Without keys React falls back to index-based matching, which can incorrectly reuse a node from a removed item. Keys give each item a stable identity so the reconciler moves rather than recreates DOM nodes.

followUpsQA.q: What is Angular's equivalent of React's reconciliation?
followUpsQA.a: Angular's change detection cycle. Ivy executes compiled instructions against the live DOM, so there is no virtual tree to diff - it updates specific bindings directly.

followUpsQA.q: Does React 18 change how the virtual DOM works?
followUpsQA.a: React 18 introduces concurrent rendering where Fiber can pause, interrupt, and restart reconciliation work, allowing the browser to stay responsive during heavy updates.

followUpsQA.q: Is the virtual DOM a performance optimisation or a developer experience choice?
followUpsQA.a: Primarily a developer experience choice - it lets you write declarative UI code that describes the full desired state. The performance characteristic is a side effect of batching DOM writes, not a fundamental speed advantage.

dialogue.text: Explain React's virtual DOM and how Angular approaches the same rendering problem differently.

React keeps an in-memory virtual DOM tree. On each state change it re-renders components into that tree, diffs the new tree against the old one through its Fiber reconciler, and writes only the changed nodes to the real DOM. Angular does not have a virtual tree at all - Ivy compiles templates into DOM instructions at build time, so at runtime it just executes those instructions against the live DOM directly.
What exactly does the Fiber reconciler do during that diff?

It walks both trees comparing element types. If the type is the same it updates the existing node in place. If the type changed it unmounts the old subtree and mounts a new one. For lists it relies on the key prop to match items by identity rather than position, which avoids incorrectly reusing a DOM node from a removed item.
You said Angular compiles to instructions - what does that look like at runtime?

Ivy produces calls like elementStart, textInterpolate, and elementEnd that are co-located with the component. When change detection runs it executes those calls and they write directly to the real DOM nodes they own. There is no shadow copy to allocate or garbage collect, which is why incremental DOM uses less memory.
So which approach is faster?

Neither is universally faster - it depends on the workload. The VDOM approach batches writes and gives a simple programming model. Angular's incremental DOM has lower memory overhead on large trees. Both reach similar real-world performance numbers on most applications.
How does React 18's concurrent mode affect reconciliation?

Concurrent mode lets Fiber split reconciliation work into chunks and pause between them to keep the browser responsive. So a heavy update no longer blocks user input - React renders in the background and commits only when ready.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What are useMemo and useRef in React, and when do you use each?

answer.oneLiner: useMemo caches an expensive computed value between renders and only recomputes it when its listed dependencies change. useRef gives you a mutable container that persists across renders without triggering a re-render when you change it.
answer.detail: React re-runs a function component on every render. Without useMemo, a costly sort or filter calculation runs every time even if the data has not changed. useMemo(fn, [deps]) stores the result and returns the cached value until one of the deps changes. It is purely a performance optimisation - the result must be the same for the same inputs, so never use it for side effects. useRef solves a different problem. The .current property lives outside React's state system, so writing to it does not schedule a re-render. The most common use is to hold a reference to a real DOM element by attaching the ref object to a JSX element's ref prop. The second common use is to hold a mutable value across renders that you do not want to display - for example a timer ID, a previous value, or an abort controller. Simplified: useMemo is for computed values, useRef is an escape hatch for a mutable box. Angular equivalents: useMemo maps to computed() signal, useRef for DOM access maps to viewChild().
keyPoints: useMemo(fn, deps) recomputes only when a dependency changes - use for expensive calculations like sorting large product lists.
useRef gives a .current box that survives renders without causing re-renders when written to.
useRef is the standard way to access a real DOM node inside a function component.
Do not overuse useMemo - wrapping trivial computations adds overhead without benefit.

code.note: useMemo caches the sort result; useRef attaches to the ul element for potential scroll manipulation without causing a re-render.

answer.analogy: useMemo is a sticky note with the answer; useRef is a notebook that React never reads over your shoulder.
redFlags: Saying useMemo is for preventing re-renders - that is useCallback and React.memo, not useMemo.
Using useState to store a value that should not trigger re-renders, like a timer ID.
Memoising a trivial calculation like string concatenation - the memoisation overhead costs more than the calculation.
Not knowing useRef is the correct way to access a DOM node in function components.

followUpsQA.q: What is the difference between useMemo and useCallback?
followUpsQA.a: useMemo memoises a computed value and returns it. useCallback memoises a function reference and returns it. useCallback(fn, deps) is equivalent to useMemo(() => fn, deps).

followUpsQA.q: Why does mutating useRef not cause a re-render?
followUpsQA.a: Because .current is a plain object property outside React's state tracking. React only re-renders when a state setter is called - ref mutations are invisible to it.

followUpsQA.q: What is the Angular equivalent of useMemo?
followUpsQA.a: A computed() signal. It is lazy and memoised - it only recomputes when a tracked signal dependency changes, which is exactly the same contract as useMemo but with automatic dependency tracking.

followUpsQA.q: When would you use useRef to store a previous value?
followUpsQA.a: When you need to compare the current prop against the previous render's value without a second state variable. Store the old value in a ref, read it on render, then update it in a useEffect.

followUpsQA.q: Can you read a ref value inside JSX to display it?
followUpsQA.a: You can technically read ref.current in JSX but it is an anti-pattern. If the value needs to appear in the UI it should be state, because changing a ref does not trigger a re-render and the display would go stale.

dialogue.text: Walk me through useMemo and useRef - what each one does and when you actually reach for it.

useMemo caches the result of an expensive computation between renders. I give it a factory function and a dependency array, and it returns the cached value until one of those deps changes. I use it when I have something like sorting a large product catalogue - without it that sort runs on every render even when the products did not change.
And useRef?

useRef gives me a .current property that lives outside React's state system. Writing to it does not schedule a re-render. I use it most often to hold a reference to a DOM element - attach the ref to a JSX element's ref prop and I can call .focus() or measure the layout without going through state.
You said useMemo caches a value - does it prevent the component from re-rendering?

No, that is a common misconception. useMemo does not prevent re-renders at all. The component still re-renders - useMemo just means the cached value is returned cheaply instead of recomputed. To prevent a child from re-rendering you would wrap it in React.memo.
What kind of value should I not put in useMemo?

Trivial computations. Concatenating two strings or adding two numbers is so fast that the memoisation bookkeeping costs more than the calculation. Also anything with side effects - useMemo must be a pure function of its deps.
How does this compare to Angular's computed signal?

Same contract but with automatic dependency tracking. Angular's computed() lazily recomputes only when a tracked signal changes. You do not declare a dependency array - Angular tracks reads automatically, so you cannot accidentally omit a dependency the way you can with useMemo's deps array.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What is React's Context API, and how does it solve deep prop passing?

answer.oneLiner: React Context lets you broadcast a value from a high-level component to any descendant that wants it, without manually passing it as a prop through every component in between. It solves prop drilling.
answer.detail: Prop drilling is when a parent passes a value to a child only so the child can pass it to its child, and so on, until it reaches the component that actually needs it. Context removes that chain. You create a context object with createContext, wrap the subtree in a Provider that supplies the value, and any descendant can read it with useContext. When the Provider's value prop changes, all consumers re-render synchronously. This is the important performance caveat: even components that use only a small part of the context object re-render when any part of the value changes. This makes Context a good fit for stable, cross-cutting values like the current user's theme preference, authentication status, or active locale. It is a poor fit for values that change on every keystroke or on every second. For high-frequency global state use a dedicated state management library like Zustand or Redux Toolkit instead, because they allow selective subscriptions. The Angular equivalent is a service provided high in the injector tree - any component below can inject it without any prop threading.
keyPoints: createContext creates the context, Provider supplies the value, useContext reads it - those three pieces make the full API.
All consumers re-render when the Provider value changes, regardless of whether they care about the changed part.
Context suits stable, infrequently changing values: theme, auth user, locale, feature flags.
For high-frequency state use a library with selective subscriptions instead of Context.

code.note: The AppShell and Sidebar no longer pass user as a prop - UserAvatar reads it directly from Context.

answer.analogy: Context is a company-wide announcement board - post once at the top, anyone can read it.
redFlags: Using Context for values that change frequently like a search query or scroll position - every consumer re-renders on each change.
Not knowing all three parts of the Context API: createContext, Provider, and useContext.
Confusing Context with a state management library - Context does not provide selective subscriptions.
Saying Context replaces Redux for all cases - it does not handle complex state transitions or middleware.

followUpsQA.q: When would you choose Zustand or Redux over Context?
followUpsQA.a: When the state changes frequently or when different consumers only care about different slices. Libraries like Zustand use selector functions to re-render a component only when its specific slice changes - Context has no such mechanism.

followUpsQA.q: How do you prevent unnecessary re-renders when a Context value is an object?
followUpsQA.a: Memoize the value with useMemo before passing it to the Provider so the object reference stays stable when its contents have not changed. If only part of the context changes, split it into multiple contexts.

followUpsQA.q: What is the Angular equivalent of React Context?
followUpsQA.a: A service provided at a high level in the injector tree using providedIn or a component-level providers array. Any descendant injects it with inject() and gets the same instance without prop threading.

followUpsQA.q: Can you have multiple contexts in one app?
followUpsQA.a: Yes, and it is encouraged. Separate concerns into separate contexts - one for auth, one for theme, one for locale - so a change to the theme does not re-render auth consumers.

followUpsQA.q: How would you unit-test a component that reads from Context?
followUpsQA.a: Wrap the component under test in a Provider with a test value during the test. This mirrors how Angular tests inject a mock service - you control the provided value in the test environment.

dialogue.text: What is React's Context API and what problem does it solve?

Context solves prop drilling - the pattern where a value gets passed from parent to child to grandchild purely as a messenger, even though intermediate components do not use it. Context lets you provide a value once at a high level and have any descendant read it directly with useContext, skipping the chain entirely.
Walk me through the three parts of the API.

createContext creates the context object and sets the default value. You wrap the relevant subtree in a Provider and pass the real value via its value prop. Any descendant calls useContext with that context object and gets the current value. When the Provider's value changes all consumers re-render.
You said all consumers re-render - is that a problem?

It is a problem for high-frequency updates. If I put a user's search query into Context, every component that reads that context re-renders on every keystroke. For stable values like auth status or theme it is fine. For frequent updates I use a library like Zustand that gives selector-based subscriptions so only the component that cares about the changed slice re-renders.
How would you handle a Context object where only part of it changes frequently?

Split it into two contexts. Put the stable data like the authenticated user in one context and the volatile data like notification count in another. That way a notification update does not re-render every component that only needs the user object.
What is the Angular equivalent of this pattern?

A root-provided service or a service provided at a specific component level. Angular's hierarchical injector tree mirrors the component tree exactly like Context's Provider subtree. The difference is Angular uses inject() rather than a hook, and services are singletons by default - there is no risk of accidentally creating two instances the way you could with two nested Providers.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅
